C:
Here's a short decision memo you can adapt. I've filled in sensible default retention periods so it's actionable as-is; the bracketed items are the calls only you/your org can make.

---

**MEMO — Decision Requested**

**To:** [Leadership / Data Governance Committee]
**From:** [Name, role]
**Date:** 2026-06-22
**Re:** Adopting a company-wide data-retention and deletion policy

**Decision requested:** Approve a formal data-retention and deletion policy that sets default retention periods by data category and mandates automated, auditable deletion when those periods lapse. Target: policy approved this quarter, enforcement live next quarter.

**Why now.** We currently retain most data indefinitely with no systematic deletion. That carries three concrete costs: rising storage and breach-blast-radius exposure, growing compliance risk under GDPR/CCPA "storage limitation" and right-to-erasure obligations, and slower discovery during audits or incidents. A clear policy converts an open-ended liability into a managed, defensible process.

**Scope.** All personal, customer, operational, and log data across production databases, backups, analytics stores, and third-party processors. Excludes [source code, anonymized/aggregated datasets — to confirm].

**Recommended retention defaults** (adjust to your legal/industry requirements):

| Data category | Default retention | Then |
|---|---|---|
| Active customer account data | Life of account + 30 days | Delete |
| Transaction / financial records | 7 years (statutory) | Delete |
| Marketing / consent data | Until consent withdrawn, max 24 mo | Delete |
| Application & access logs | 90 days | Delete |
| Analytics events (raw) | 14 months | Anonymize or delete |
| Backups | 35 days rolling | Auto-expire |
| Ex-employee HR records | Per local labor law [confirm] | Delete |

**Key policy decisions to ratify:**
1. **Defaults over case-by-case** — retention is set per category, not per team, to keep it enforceable.
2. **Deletion is automated and logged** — scheduled jobs delete on expiry; every deletion writes an audit record (what, when, by which rule). No silent manual purges.
3. **Legal hold overrides deletion** — a documented hold process suspends deletion for data under litigation/investigation.
4. **Backups expire on a fixed window** — so deleted records don't persist indefinitely in restores.
5. **Single owner** — [Data Protection Officer / named role] owns the policy, the category list, and annual review.

**Options considered.** (a) *Status quo / ad-hoc* — lowest effort, highest long-term liability; rejected. (b) *Manual periodic purges* — better, but error-prone and hard to prove in an audit; rejected as the primary mechanism. (c) *Policy + automated enforcement* — recommended; higher upfront engineering cost, but defensible and self-sustaining.

**Implementation plan.**
- *Phase 1 (weeks 1–3):* Inventory data stores and map each to a category; ratify the table above.
- *Phase 2 (weeks 4–8):* Build deletion jobs + audit logging for the two highest-risk categories (logs, marketing data).
- *Phase 3 (weeks 9–12):* Extend to remaining categories, wire up legal-hold workflow, update the privacy notice and processor contracts.
- *Ongoing:* Annual review; deletion-audit report to the committee each quarter.

**Risks / open questions.**
- Statutory minimums vary by jurisdiction — confirm the financial and HR rows with legal before enforcement.
- Hard deletes can break referential integrity or downstream analytics — Phase 2 must verify on staging first.
- Third-party processors must honor the same windows — needs contract review.

**Ask:** Approve the policy direction and the Phase 1 inventory so we can return next month with finalized retention periods and an enforcement design.

---

Two things worth flagging before you circulate it: the financial (7-year) and HR rows are placeholders for statutory minimums that depend on your jurisdiction and industry — have legal confirm them, since under-retaining regulated records is as risky as over-retaining. If you tell me your industry, jurisdiction, and main data stores, I can tighten the table and scope to your actual situation. I can also save this to your documents folder if you want it as a file.
